pin_title: Milk Beats Greek Yogurt on Protein per Dollar: 29 vs 28
pin_description: Plain milk edges out Greek yogurt on protein per dollar, 29 to 28. The boring carton wins. We ranked 6 dairy staples with USDA numbers and one store's prices, and the fancy tubs don't come out on top.
alt_text: Playful illustration of a milk carton winning an arm wrestle against a Greek yogurt tub, with a badge reading 29 vs 28 protein per dollar.

pin_title: Eggs Are Only #19 of 49 on Protein per Dollar
pin_description: Eggs are good. They're just not the budget champion everyone repeats: 19th of 49 in our protein-per-dollar ranking, and every dried bean beats them. See what actually tops the list, priced at one store with USDA numbers.
alt_text: Playful illustration of a shocked cartoon egg holding up a card reading number 19, surrounded by confetti.

pin_title: The Cheapest High-Fiber Snack Is Popcorn: 58g per Dollar
pin_description: Popcorn kernels deliver 58 grams of fiber per dollar, the best snack math in the store. Chips never stood a chance. We ranked 10 snacks with USDA data and real prices, and the podium isn't what you'd guess.
alt_text: Playful illustration of a happy popcorn kernel wearing a golden crown, with a badge reading 58g per dollar.

pin_title: Tofu Is the Fancy One: 14g of Protein per Dollar vs Lentils at 78
pin_description: Plant protein has a pecking order. Tofu lands at 14 grams of protein per dollar while humble lentils deliver 78. We ranked 18 plant proteins with USDA data and one store's prices. Beans run the show, and it isn't close.
alt_text: Playful illustration of a tofu block with a monocle and top hat on a pedestal, with a cheerful crowd of lentils and beans below.

pin_title: Frozen Beats Fresh: Peas Deliver 18g of Fiber per Dollar
pin_description: Frozen green peas beat every fresh vegetable we priced on fiber per dollar: 18 grams. The freezer aisle doesn't photograph well, but it wins on math. All 22 fruits and vegetables ranked with USDA data and real store prices.
alt_text: Playful illustration of a bag of frozen peas wearing sunglasses next to a surprised apple, with a badge reading 18g of fiber per dollar.

pin_title: Bacon Finished Dead Last: 9g of Protein per Dollar (We Priced 49 Foods)
pin_description: Bacon came in dead last in our 49-food protein ranking: 9 grams per dollar. Pinto beans deliver 98. Same store, same month, USDA numbers, and the full list settles a lot of grocery arguments. See where your usual buys actually rank.
alt_text: Playful illustration of a sad cartoon bacon strip on the floor below a podium of celebrating beans, with a badge reading 9g of protein per dollar.

pin_title: Flour Is Secretly a Protein Powerhouse: 96g per Dollar
pin_description: Whole wheat flour took second place out of 49 foods in our protein-per-dollar ranking: 96 grams per dollar. Nobody brags about flour, but the math doesn't care about bragging. See the full ranking and what else quietly embarrasses the meat aisle.
alt_text: Playful illustration of a smiling flour bag wearing a superhero cape flying through clouds, with a badge reading 96g per dollar.

pin_title: A Full Day of Protein for 82 Cents
pin_description: We priced five real days that each hit 50 grams of protein. The cheapest came out to 82 cents: oats, split peas, pintos, rice, and peanut butter. The same protein from the drive-thru runs $9.97. Every meal is priced out on the site.
alt_text: Playful illustration of a smiling piggy bank with a coin, surrounded by a bowl of beans, eggs, and oats.
